source: （中英）润色后-故宫博物院导览词.docx
paragraphs: 故宫博物院 / The Palace Museum (Forbidden City)
开场白：故宫博物院 / Intro: The Palace Museum
大家早上好！欢迎开启这段精彩的旅程。我是你们的导游米娅，非常荣幸能在今天陪伴大家。咱们此刻正站在故宫的正门——午门脚下！眼前这"凹"字形的门楼，黄瓦红墙、檐角飞翘，像五只展翅的凤凰，所以老北京人都叫它"五凤楼"。咱们就从这儿迈进去，沿着故宫的中轴线，一步步逛遍皇帝的"金銮殿"、后妃的寝宫，感受这座600年皇家宫殿的气派与智慧。咱们抬脚进午门，出发！

Good morning, everyone, and welcome! I'm Miya, your tour guide for today, and I'm so glad to have you all with me. Right now, we're standing at the foot of the Meridian Gate—the grand main entrance to the Palace Museum. Take a look at that gate tower up ahead: it's shaped like a concave "U" with yellow glazed tiles, red walls, and sweeping eaves that curve upward like the wings of five phoenixes in flight. That's why old Beijingers call it the "Tower of Five Phoenixes." From here, we'll walk along the central axis of the palace, visiting the emperor's grand throne halls and the private quarters of the imperial family, step by step. Ready? Let's head inside!
一、午门：故宫正门与历史沿革 / I. Meridian Gate: Main Entrance & Historical Evolution
刚进午门，先跟大家聊聊故宫的"身世"——它最早叫"紫禁城"，为啥叫这名？古代天文学家认为"紫微星垣"是天帝住的地方，皇帝是"天子"，住的地方就得对应"紫宫"；再加上这儿是皇家禁地，老百姓不准进，所以叫"紫禁城"。

Now that we're through the Meridian Gate, let me start with a bit of history. The Palace Museum was originally known as the "Purple Forbidden City"—or Zijincheng in Chinese. The name comes from ancient Chinese astronomy: astronomers believed that the "Purple Star Enclosure" in the night sky was the heavenly residence of the Celestial Emperor. Since the earthly emperor was considered the "Son of Heaven," his palace had to mirror that celestial home. On top of that, this was a strictly forbidden area—ordinary people weren't allowed anywhere near it. Put those two ideas together, and you get "Purple Forbidden City."
紫禁城是明成祖朱棣下令建的，从1406年动工，花了14年到1420年建成。为啥要在北京建皇宫？因为朱棣之前被封为"燕王"，根基在北方，迁都北京既能巩固北方边防，又方便他统治全国。1925年，这里正式改成"故宫博物院"，从此皇家宫殿变成了全中国人都能参观的文化宝库。

Construction was ordered by Emperor Yongle—also known as Zhu Di—in 1406 and took 14 years, finally wrapping up in 1420. Why Beijing? Well, before becoming emperor, Zhu Di held the title "Prince of Yan" and had spent years building his power base in the north. Moving the capital here let him keep a closer eye on the northern frontier while strengthening his grip on the entire empire. Fast forward to 1925, and the palace was officially renamed the "Palace Museum," opening its doors to the public for the first time and transforming from an exclusive imperial compound into a cultural treasure for all Chinese people.
咱们再看眼前的故宫格局：它占地72万平方米，相当于100个足球场，有9000多间房屋；整个宫殿呈长方形，长961米、宽753米，外围是10米高的红墙，墙外面绕着52米宽的"筒子河"（护城河），既防敌人进攻，又能排水防潮。四角还有4座角楼，每座角楼都有"九梁十八柱七十二脊"，没用一根钉子，全靠木榫卯拼接，是中国古代建筑的"活化石"。

Let me give you a sense of the scale. The palace grounds cover 720,000 square meters—roughly the size of 100 football fields—with over 9,000 rooms. The entire complex is rectangular: 961 meters from north to south and 753 meters from east to west. It's surrounded by a 10-meter-high red wall, and just outside that wall flows the Tongzi River, a 52-meter-wide moat that served both as a defensive barrier and a drainage system. At each of the four corners, you'll find a corner tower famed for its intricate structure—"nine beams, eighteen columns, and seventy-two ridges"—assembled entirely using traditional mortise-and-tenon joinery without a single iron nail. These towers are essentially living fossils of ancient Chinese woodworking.
二、太和门：前朝的"门户"与整体布局 / II. Gate of Supreme Harmony: Gateway to the Outer Court & Overall Layout
走过午门，前面这座气派的门楼就是太和门——咱们从这儿开始，就进入故宫的"前朝"区域了（皇帝处理朝政的地方）。故宫整体按"前朝后寝"布局：南边是"前朝"（外朝），北边是"后寝"（内廷，皇帝后妃生活的地方），中间用乾清门隔开，特别规整。

Just past the Meridian Gate, you can see an impressive gate tower straight ahead—that's the Gate of Supreme Harmony, and it marks our entry into the "Outer Court," the part of the palace where the emperor conducted official state business. The Forbidden City follows a clear "front court, rear living quarters" layout. The southern half is the Outer Court, devoted to governance and ceremony, while the northern half is the Inner Court, where the emperor and his family actually lived. The two areas are separated by the Gate of Heavenly Purity right in the middle. It's a very orderly arrangement.
先给大家梳理下中轴线的主要建筑：咱们现在走的这条南北线，从午门开始，经太和门、太和殿、中和殿、保和殿，再到乾清门、乾清宫、交泰殿、坤宁宫，最后到御花园、神武门，这是故宫的"脊梁"，所有重要建筑都沿着它排列。

Let me walk you through the key buildings along the central axis—the north-south spine of the entire complex. Starting from the Meridian Gate, the line runs through the Gate of Supreme Harmony, the Hall of Supreme Harmony, the Hall of Central Harmony, and the Hall of Preserving Harmony. Then it continues past the Gate of Heavenly Purity into the Inner Court, passing the Palace of Heavenly Purity, the Hall of Union, and the Palace of Earthly Tranquility, before ending at the Imperial Garden and finally the Gate of Divine Prowess in the north. This axis is the backbone of the Forbidden City, and every major building lines up along it.

除了中轴线，两边还有"两翼"：前朝的东边是文华殿（过去是太子读书、存放《四库全书》的地方），西边是武英殿（皇帝处理军务、接见武将的地方）；后寝的东边是"东六宫"（景仁宫、承乾宫、钟粹宫、景阳宫、永和宫、延禧宫，后妃住的地方），西边是"西六宫"（永寿宫、翊坤宫、储秀宫、咸福宫、长春宫、启祥宫）；再往外，东边有"外东路"（宁寿宫区，乾隆皇帝为养老专门建的，里面有珍妃井），西边有"外西路"（慈宁宫区，太后、太妃们的居所）。

Flanking the central axis are two "wings" of buildings. In the Outer Court, the Hall of Literary Glory sits to the east—once used by the crown prince for his studies and later as a repository for the Siku Quanshu, an enormous imperial encyclopedia. To the west is the Hall of Martial Valor, where the emperor handled military affairs and received his generals. In the Inner Court, the east side holds the Six Eastern Palaces—Jingren, Chengqian, Zhongcui, Jingyang, Yonghe, and Yanxi—which served as residences for the imperial concubines. The west side mirrors this with the Six Western Palaces: Yongshou, Yikun, Chuxiu, Xianfu, Changchun, and Qixiang. Beyond those, on the far east, lies the Outer East Road, home to the Ningshou Palace area, which Emperor Qianlong had built as his retirement retreat (and where you'll find the famous Well of Concubine Zhen). On the far west is the Outer West Road, centered on the Cining Palace area, where empresses dowager and senior imperial women spent their later years.
三、外朝三大殿：皇帝的"政务核心区" / III. Three Great Halls of the Outer Court: The Emperor's Political Core
穿过太和门，咱们就到了太和殿广场，眼前这座金灿灿的大殿，就是故宫的"C位"——太和殿，俗称"金銮殿"。

Through the Gate of Supreme Harmony, we step out into the vast Hall of Supreme Harmony Square. That gleaming golden structure dead ahead is the absolute centerpiece of the Forbidden City—the Hall of Supreme Harmony, popularly known as the "Throne of Golden Excellence."
太和殿 / Hall of Supreme Harmony
太和殿是故宫等级最高的建筑，你看它的屋顶——"重檐庑殿顶"，这种屋顶只有皇宫、太庙才能用，屋脊上有10只瑞兽（从龙到行什），全国独一份！大殿高35米，面阔11间，进深5间。

This is the highest-ranking building in the entire palace. Check out the roof: it's a "double-eaved hip roof"—a style that, in imperial times, was reserved exclusively for the emperor's palaces and ancestral temples. And count the mythical beasts lining the ridge: there are ten of them, from the dragon all the way to hangshi, a legendary creature you won't find on any other roof in China. The hall itself stands 35 meters tall, stretching 11 bays across and 5 bays deep.
走进殿内，正中是金漆雕龙宝座，扶手、靠背全是蟠龙花纹，上面盖着明黄色龙袍式的椅套；宝座上方挂着"建极绥猷（jiàn jí suí yóu）"，是乾隆帝写的。过去皇帝登基、大婚、元旦朝贺、册立皇后这些重大典礼，都在这儿举行——想象一下，文武百官跪拜在广场上，礼乐声震耳欲聋，多气派！地面铺的"金砖"也很特别，是苏州澄泥烧制的，敲着像金石响，一块砖值一两白银呢。

Step inside, and right in the center you'll see the golden lacquered dragon throne. The armrests and backrest are covered in intricately carved coiled dragons, draped with a bright yellow cover patterned like an imperial robe. Suspended above the throne is a plaque bearing four characters—"Jianji Suiyou"—written by Emperor Qianlong, urging the ruler to establish the highest standard of governance. This is where the most important ceremonies took place: imperial ascensions, royal weddings, New Year's court gatherings, and the formal installation of empresses. Picture the scene—hundreds of civil and military officials kneeling in the square below, ceremonial music echoing off the walls. It must have been breathtaking. Even the floor is extraordinary: it's paved with so-called "golden bricks" fired from fine clay in Suzhou. Tap one and it rings like metal. In those days, a single brick was said to be worth one tael of silver.
中和殿 / Hall of Central Harmony
太和殿后面的中和殿，就小巧多了，屋顶是"四角攒尖顶"，宝顶鎏金，像个金色的小宝塔。这儿是皇帝大典前的"休息室"——比如要在太和殿举行仪式，皇帝先在这儿喝杯茶、整理龙袍，再接受大臣的朝拜。殿内的宝座比太和殿小，但雕工更细，上方"允执厥中"匾额是乾隆皇帝写的，意思是治国要守"中庸之道"。

Right behind the Hall of Supreme Harmony sits the much smaller Hall of Central Harmony. Its roof is a "pyramidal" style with a gilded finial on top, giving it the look of a little golden pagoda. Think of this as the emperor's green room before big ceremonies. Before appearing at the Hall of Supreme Harmony, the emperor would come here to have some tea, straighten his robes, and receive formal greetings from his ministers. The throne inside is more modest in size than the one next door, but the carvings are arguably more refined. The plaque above—"Yunzhi Juezhong"—was also inscribed by Emperor Qianlong and carries the message that a ruler should govern by adhering to the Golden Mean.
保和殿 / Hall of Preserving Harmony
最后这座保和殿，屋顶是"重檐歇山顶"，比太和殿少了点威严，多了些灵动。它的用途很广：明朝时皇帝在这儿宴请藩国使臣；清朝乾隆以后，每三年一次的"殿试"（全国最高级考试）也在这儿举行——全国最优秀的读书人在这儿考一天，考中就能当"进士"，所以这儿也叫"天子堂"。殿内"皇建有极"匾额，意思是皇权要立规矩、守准则。

The last of the three great halls is the Hall of Preserving Harmony, crowned with a "double-eaved gable-and-hip roof"—a touch less imposing than the Hall of Supreme Harmony but with its own graceful character. Over the centuries, it served a variety of purposes. During the Ming Dynasty, the emperor hosted banquets here for envoys from tributary states. Starting in the Qianlong period of the Qing Dynasty, the triennial Palace Examination—the final and most prestigious stage of the imperial civil service exam—was held right here. The top scholars from across the empire would spend a day writing their essays under this roof, and those who passed earned the title of jinshi, or "imperial scholar." No wonder people also called it the "Hall of the Son of Heaven." The plaque inside reads "Huangjian Youji," reminding the emperor that imperial power must be exercised according to established principles and laws.
四、乾清门与内廷后三宫：皇帝的"家" / IV. Inner Court: The Emperor's Private World
走过保和殿，前面那座门就是乾清门——咱们从这儿跨过门槛，就从"前朝"走进"后寝"了，气氛一下子从庄严变得温馨，这是皇帝和后妃生活的地方。

Beyond the Hall of Preserving Harmony, we come to the Gate of Heavenly Purity. Cross this threshold, and we leave the formal grandeur of the Outer Court behind and enter the Inner Court—the emperor's actual home. The atmosphere shifts from imposing to intimate. This is where the imperial family lived, dined, and spent their private hours.
乾清宫 / Palace of Heavenly Purity
乾清宫是明朝和清初皇帝的寝宫，直到雍正皇帝搬到养心殿。大家看殿内的"正大光明"匾额（和太和殿的不一样，是康熙皇帝重写的），雍正以后，皇帝会把传位诏书藏在匾额后面，等驾崩后再拿出来，避免皇子争位——这就是"秘密立储"制度，特别聪明。

The Palace of Heavenly Purity served as the emperor's bedroom from the Ming Dynasty through the early Qing, right up until Emperor Yongzheng moved his residence to the Hall of Mental Cultivation. Take a look at the plaque hanging inside: "Zhengda Guangming"—"Upright and Bright." This one was written by Emperor Kangxi, and it played a fascinating role in Qing Dynasty politics. Starting with Yongzheng, each emperor would secretly name his successor in a sealed edict and hide it behind this very plaque. The document would only be opened after the emperor's death, preventing any scheming among the princes over who would inherit the throne. It was an ingenious system known as "secret imperial succession."
宝座两侧摆着"江山""社稷"宝鼎，象征皇帝掌管天下；地面的万字纹地砖被踩了几百年，锃亮锃亮的，全是岁月的痕迹。过去皇帝也会在这儿处理日常政务，比如批阅奏折、召见大臣，既是寝宫也是"小办公室"。

Flanking the throne are two bronze tripods representing "the Land" and "the Grain"—symbols of the emperor's dominion over all under heaven. Look down at the swastika-patterned floor tiles: they've been walked on for centuries and now gleam with a deep, glassy polish, each scratch and scuff a mark of time. This hall wasn't just a bedroom; it also functioned as the emperor's daily office, where he reviewed memorials, met with ministers, and handled the routine business of running an empire.
交泰殿 / Hall of Union
交泰殿在乾清宫和坤宁宫中间，名字取"天地交泰"，象征"天（皇帝）地（皇后）和谐"。这儿是皇后的"专属空间"：每年冬至、元旦，皇后会在这儿接受妃嫔、公主的朝拜；殿里最珍贵的是25方"传国玉玺"，每方用途不同——"皇帝之宝"盖诏书，"制诰之宝"颁法令，这可是皇权的象征。

Tucked between the Palace of Heavenly Purity and the Palace of Earthly Tranquility is the Hall of Union. Its name comes from the idea of "heaven and earth in harmony"—symbolizing the complementary relationship between the emperor (heaven) and the empress (earth). This was very much the empress's domain. Every year on the Winter Solstice and on New Year's Day, she would receive formal respects from the concubines and princesses right here. But the real treasures are kept inside: twenty-five imperial seals, each one used for a different purpose. The "Emperor's Treasure" was for stamping edicts, the "Edict Treasure" for promulgating laws, and so on. Together, they were the ultimate symbols of imperial authority.
坤宁宫 / Palace of Earthly Tranquility
坤宁宫原本是皇后的寝宫，清朝入关后改了用途：东边一半是皇帝的婚房，比如光绪皇帝大婚就在这儿，现在还能看到红漆家具、墙上的喜字，特别喜庆；西边一半是萨满教祭祀场所，供桌上摆着青铜祭器，梁上挂着祭祀用的铃铛，满汉文化在这儿融合得特别明显。

The Palace of Earthly Tranquility was originally the empress's bedroom, but after the Qing Dynasty took over, its function changed dramatically. The eastern half was converted into the emperor's bridal chamber. Emperor Guangxu's wedding, for instance, was celebrated here, and you can still see the red-lacquered furniture and paper "double happiness" characters on the walls—it's wonderfully festive. The western half, on the other hand, became a space for Shamanistic rituals, reflecting the Manchu heritage of the Qing emperors. Bronze ritual vessels sit on the altar, and ceremonial bells hang from the beams. It's one of the clearest examples of how Manchu and Han cultures blended within the palace walls.
五、御花园与神武门：故宫的"后花园"与终点 / V. Imperial Garden & Gate of Divine Prowess
逛完后三宫，北边就是御花园——这是皇帝、后妃休闲散心的地方，虽然面积不大（只有1.2万平方米），但处处是景。

After the Three Back Palaces, we reach the Imperial Garden to the north. This was the imperial family's backyard—a place to unwind and enjoy nature. It's not especially large, only about 12,000 square meters, but every corner has something worth looking at.
御花园按中轴对称布局，中心是钦安殿（供奉真武大帝，过去皇帝会在这儿祭拜，祈求平安）；东边有堆秀山，用太湖石堆成，山顶的御景亭能俯瞰半个故宫；西边有延辉阁，旁边是几百年的古柏，其中一棵"连理枝"特别有名，象征"夫妻和睦"。园子里还有各种奇石、花木，比如石榴树（象征多子多福）、海棠花（象征富贵吉祥），走在这儿，一点没有皇宫的严肃，反而像逛江南小院。

The garden is laid out symmetrically along the central axis. At its heart stands the Hall of Imperial Peace, dedicated to the True Warrior Emperor of Daoist tradition, where emperors once came to pray for safety and good fortune. To the east rises the Hill of Accumulated Elegance, a man-made peak built from Taihu limestone rocks. Climb to the Imperial View Pavilion at the top, and you can see out over roughly half the Forbidden City. On the west side sits the Pavilion of Spreading Glory, surrounded by ancient cypress trees—some of them centuries old. One particularly famous specimen is a "twin trunk tree," two trunks growing from a single root, traditionally seen as a symbol of marital harmony. The garden is also filled with ornamental rocks, pomegranate trees (symbolizing fertility and many children), and crabapple blossoms (representing wealth and good fortune). Strolling through here, you'd hardly guess you were still inside an imperial palace—it feels more like a classical garden from southern China.
穿过御花园，咱们就到了故宫的北门——神武门，原来叫"玄武门"，后来为了避康熙皇帝（玄烨）的讳，改成了"神武门"。过去皇帝出巡、打猎会从这儿走；现在，这儿是故宫的出口，咱们的游览也快到终点了。

Beyond the garden lies the north gate of the Forbidden City—the Gate of Divine Prowess. Its original name was actually the "Black Warrior Gate" (Xuanwu Gate), but it had to be changed because the character "Xuan" appeared in the personal name of Emperor Kangxi, and naming taboos were taken very seriously in those days. In imperial times, the emperor would pass through this gate when heading out on tours or hunting expeditions. Today, it's the main exit, and our tour is nearly complete.
对了，还要跟大家说一句：1987年，故宫被联合国教科文组织列入《世界遗产名录》，评价它是"中国古代宫殿建筑的集大成者"，这份荣誉，是对这座600年宫殿最好的认可。

One last thing before we go: in 1987, the Forbidden City was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, hailed as "the culmination of ancient Chinese palace architecture." For a 600-year-old complex, that's about as good an endorsement as you can get.
欢送词 / Farewell Speech
各位朋友，咱们从午门进，到神武门出，故宫之旅就结束啦！咱们逛了太和殿的金銮宝座，看了乾清宫的"正大光明"匾，走了御花园的石子路，每一步都踩着历史的痕迹。这座宫殿不只是黄瓦红墙的建筑，更是一本写满中国故事的书——有皇帝的治国智慧，有工匠的建筑巧思，还有无数普通人的生活痕迹。希望今天的讲解，能让大家记住故宫的美，也欢迎大家以后再来，慢慢品味更多藏在红墙里的秘密。感谢大家的陪伴，祝大家旅途顺利，再见！

Well, everyone, that brings our Forbidden City tour to a close! We came in through the Meridian Gate and now we're leaving through the Gate of Divine Prowess. Along the way, we stood before the golden throne in the Hall of Supreme Harmony, gazed up at the "Upright and Bright" plaque in the Palace of Heavenly Purity, and wandered the winding paths of the Imperial Garden. Every step we took was a step through history. This palace is so much more than yellow tiles and red walls—it's a book filled with stories of China: the political wisdom of its emperors, the ingenuity of its craftsmen, and the quiet lives of countless ordinary people who kept it all running. I hope today's tour gave you a real sense of the Forbidden City's beauty and significance. You're always welcome back—there are plenty more secrets hiding behind these red walls. Thank you so much for spending the day with me. Have a wonderful rest of your trip, and goodbye!
